title: Floor Work: Where Adults Forget the Floor Exists
dek: You stopped sitting on the floor somewhere around your twelfth birthday, trading the solid ground for chairs that compress your spine and trap your fury. It is time to remember the gravity that holds you.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Ground We Abandoned

You haven’t sat on the floor in years. Maybe decades. The last time your skin pressed against cold tile or worn wood, your knee popped in a way that echoed through the quiet house. You remember the ache trailing down your hip, the sharp pinch in your lower back that didn’t quite settle for days. So you stopped. Chairs and sofas, cushions and benches—these became the new normal, the safe perches that kept your legs folded neatly, your hips locked out of the equation.

But the floor—the solid, unyielding earth beneath you—waits. It remembers you, even if you don’t remember it. The floor is not just a surface; it’s the first skin of your body’s relationship to gravity, to your own weight, to the tension and release that happens when your pelvis and legs fold into the earth’s embrace. When you stop sitting on the floor, your psoas muscle grows tight, bristling like a rat in a trap. It’s the muscle of grounding, the deep hip flexor that connects your lumbar spine to the inside of your femur. It’s the muscle that says no when you say yes to every demand, the muscle that locks down when you’re unwilling to settle into your own center.

You don’t notice the slow crawl of tension through your levator scapulae and trapezius until the weight of your day sits heavy on your shoulders, pulling your neck into that familiar, grinding knot. But the root begins deeper. Your hips, stiff and unyielding, have forgotten what it means to fold fully. The iliopsoas whispers a warning every time you reach to tie a shoe or bend to pick up a dropped cup. Your diaphragm, caught between ribs and lungs, tightens in sympathy, shrinking your breath. You hold your fury somewhere in these depths—the body knows better than your mind that anger is something to land into, something to root into, not something to run from or flatten.

You were taught that anger was ugly, that your fury was a crack in the porcelain you needed to keep pristine. So you sit on chairs, keeping your legs crossed or dangling, your pelvis tilted forward or back, but never fully settled. The floor does not judge you. It does not recoil from your sharp edges or your heat. It accepts the full weight of your body, your presence. When you come back to the floor, your body remembers what it means to be held by gravity, to be supported by something unyielding yet expansive.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma and rage live in the body—specifically in the fascia and muscles that carry our histories. The floor is a medicine here, a contact point for your angry cells to soften, to feel safe enough to shift and release. This is not a metaphor. The muscles and connective tissue around your hips and pelvis have a memory. They remember the times you held your breath, the times you clamped your jaw, the times you rushed past yourself without a pause.

Floor work is a return to that memory. It is the recognition that your body’s architecture craves the grounding force of the earth below—especially when your mind wants to run, to hide, or to numb out. So tonight, when the clock ticks past 10 and the air smells like rain on hot concrete, consider kneeling down. Let your psoas stretch open slowly, the back of your thigh pressing gently into the floor. Let your pelvis find a neutral place, neither tucking nor arching, but resting. Let your anger find its root in the earth beneath you.

This is where adults forget the floor exists. And it is where your body begins to come home.

## Gravity and the Psoas

You settle down onto the cold floor at midnight, the wood cool through your thin cotton pants, the faint scent of old pine mingling with the musk of your own skin—a little damp, a little electric. Gravity shifts in a way it never does when you’re perched in a chair or curled on a couch. The floor is steady, unyielding, and yet, you feel something strange: a slow, stubborn release, a shift in the deep muscles you didn’t know were holding so much until they don’t have to.

The psoas, that deep core muscle often called the “muscle of the soul,” is the silent reservoir of your unspoken fury. Nestled close to your lumbar spine, it wraps around your vertebrae like a secret knot. It’s no coincidence that the psoas is intimately involved in the body’s fight-or-flight response. Bessel van der Kolk, in his work on trauma, reminds us that trauma lodges itself in the body, not as a story we can always tell, but as tension, as contraction, as unyielding grip in muscles like the psoas. Here is where rage, unexpressed and uncared for, waits—coiled and ready, but locked down by the very biology designed to protect you.

You can feel it now, if you tune in. The floor beneath you invites surrender to gravity’s pull, but the psoas resists. It’s holding you upright even while you rest, a subtle contraction that braces your spine, your hips, your sense of self against a world that told you your anger was ugly, shameful, dangerous. The psoas says no to that story. It holds the tension of your survival, the weight of your rage that never found a voice.

This is not pathology. This is biology. The body knows what the mind cannot always translate. The psoas remembers what your jaw tries to clamp down—the masseter, tight and clenched, jawbone grinding in the quiet hours when the world expects you to be small and silent. Both muscles, the psoas deep in your core and the masseter around your face, are battlegrounds for unexpressed fury. They contract in synchrony, a body-wide message of “hold it in, don’t let it out,” passed down through years of conditioning.

When you lie down on the floor, you give gravity permission to do its work. Gravity becomes an ally, a gentle insistence that you can release. You feel the subtle lengthening in the psoas, the loosening of the masseter’s grip as your body remembers—muscle by muscle—that it doesn’t have to carry this rage alone. The floor, steady and unmoving, invites you to soften into what you’ve held tight for so long.

Karla McLaren teaches that anger is a messenger, a vital signal from the body, not a moral failing to be hidden away. The psoas listens to that message. It waits for your permission to unwind, to move with the pull of the earth beneath you. When you stop fighting gravity, when you stop fighting yourself, tension can begin to dissolve. You don’t have to chew on your fury or clutch it close to your spine. The body wants to remember its own strength, its own capacity to hold and then release.

The floor is where you rediscover that truth: the body remembers, the muscles remember, and you can let gravity help you carry what you thought was unbearable. This is the work adults forget—that the floor exists not just beneath your feet, but beneath your resistance, beneath your rage, holding you steady when the world demands you be anything but.

## Roots for the Rootless

You forget the floor exists until your body forces the memory back. That sudden stabbing in the sacrum when you’ve been sitting too long, the dull ache behind your knees, the way your calves cramp when the day’s tension finally unwinds at midnight—these are the body’s way of insisting you acknowledge the ground beneath you. Your nervous system doesn’t just hold anger in abstract; it clamps it tight in muscles, fascia, and bone, especially in the lower half of your body. The psoas, the adductors, the pelvic floor—these are the battlegrounds where your fury hides, clenched and unexpressed. This is not pathology, this is biology.

When anger lodges in the pelvis and legs, it’s often a kind of rootlessness. Maybe your ancestors never allowed you to feel stable, maybe your nervous system learned early that standing firm meant danger. The floor, for you, is not a place of safety but a reminder of instability. You clutch your calves, your feet curl, and the trapezius tenses to compensate. You lose connection to the ground, and with it, the ability to release what’s stuck inside the masseter or levator scapulae. Your body is screaming for a safe way to discharge that energy.

This is where herbs step in—not as a numbing agent or a quick fix, but as allies that speak the language of your nervous system. Ashwagandha, for example, is a powerhouse adaptogen that works through the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, gently downshifting your stress response. It doesn’t sedate; it strengthens. You can feel it in the slow unwinding of the psoas, the softening around your lower ribs where your diaphragm anchors. Ashwagandha helps your body remember how to breathe into the ground, how to hold presence in your pelvis without bracing. You might try [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20), taken as a tea or tincture, especially on those nights when your legs twitch in restless anger.

Skullcap is another herb that meets your nervous system where it’s jittery and on edge. The lower body’s tension often pairs with a racing mind or a heart that pounds in the quiet hours. Skullcap’s nervine qualities soothe the nerve endings tangled in your fascia, easing the levator scapulae and the hamstrings that tighten when you hold your fury inside. It’s not about erasing anger but giving your body permission to relax enough to release it. A tincture like [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) taken before floor work—the slow, grounded practice of reconnecting with your body—can be a quiet invitation for your nervous system to soften.

Cramp Bark deserves a place on this altar of root healing. This herb, traditionally used for uterine cramps and muscle spasms, speaks directly to the body’s deep muscular tension. The pelvic floor is a cradle of unspoken rage, clenching in response to trauma or shame. Cramp Bark works through smooth muscle relaxation, easing the clenching without dulling sensation. When your levator ani or iliococcygeus tighten so hard it feels like your fury might fracture your bones, a cup of Cramp Bark infusion poured warm, or a carefully measured tincture like [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20), can untangle the knot without shame or resistance.

The floor remembers, even when you don’t. These herbs don’t erase your anger or make you more palatable to the world; they root you in your biology so you can feel the grit and power of your fury without being consumed by it. Your body knows how to release. It’s just waiting for you to stop floating and start pressing down, to let the earth hold the weight you’ve carried for too long. The herbs are there to support that surrender—the one that feels like power, not defeat.

## An Embodied Practice: Meeting the Floor

You’ve forgotten the floor exists. Not because you meant to, but because your body has been busy—bracing, tensing, holding you up like a fortress. The floor is a place your adult self rarely visits, even though it’s the ground beneath your fury, the earth beneath the tightness in your trapezius, the base your psoas remembers when it finally lets go. Resmaa Menakem teaches that trauma lives in the body, and healing begins with somatic settling—finding your way back into your own flesh, back to the safety of your skin. The floor is the most honest place you have for that.

Tonight, the air is cool, edging into that dampness that clings to skin when the sun slips away too early. The floor beneath you, maybe hardwood or cold tile, presses steady and unyielding. You can smell the faint trace of cedar polish or old wool carpet, something ancient and grounding. This is where you start.

Find a spot where you can lie down, away from the buzz of screens and the pull of deadlines. Slowly, like you’re lowering a fragile treasure, ease yourself down. Notice how your calves brush against the floor first, the backs of your knees resting flat, your hamstrings lengthening. Feel the subtle resistance as your ischial tuberosities—the sit bones—sink toward the floor, heavy but supported. This is the moment your nervous system has been waiting for: the soft surrender of weight onto something steady.

As your back opens to the floor, feel the contact beneath your sacrum, the slight press into the lumbar spine. Your erector spinae muscles, those long ribbons of muscle lining your spine, begin to release their habitual grip. The diaphragm, tucked under the ribs like a quiet bellows, softens as the floor supports your whole ribcage. Breathe here. Inhale through the nose, noticing the cool air filling the belly and lower ribs. Exhale through parted lips, letting the breath fall out like a slow, warm sigh against the floor’s chill.

Your masseter muscles—those clenched, stubborn jaw muscles—may twitch or tremble. Let them. Let the jaw soften, the mouth part just enough so your tongue can rest heavy on the bottom teeth. The tension held in your levator scapulae, that tiny muscle hitching your shoulders toward your ears, begins to ease. The floor is not an adversary; it is an ally, a witness to the unwinding of all this armor.

If anger still simmers—a heat rising low in your belly—place one hand there, over your lower abdomen. The skin is warm, a contrast to the cool floor beneath your back. Let your fingers feel the subtle pulse of your belly’s movement with each breath, the slow expansion and contraction like a quiet river beneath your fingertips. This hand is a messenger, inviting you to notice what’s alive, what’s moving, what still wants to be seen.

Stay here as long as you can. Even five minutes matter. You are practicing somatic settling, the art of letting your body know it is safe to rest, to let go of the fight and flight. You are reclaiming your body’s wisdom, the primal memory that the floor will hold you, that you need not carry your fury alone in clenched fists or a tight neck.

When you finally rise, do it with intention. Roll gently to your side, pressing your hands into the floor, and feel the connection from fingertips through the wrist, elbow, shoulder—the chain of support. Push up slowly, remembering the floor beneath your feet, the earth beneath your skin, the body that carries your anger without shame.

This is not about perfection or grace. This is about returning, again and again, to the body’s truth: the floor waits. The body remembers. You are home.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: Why do I feel so awkward or even embarrassed when I try to sit or lie on the floor?**  
A: Because somewhere along the line, you learned the floor was for kids or for being down, not for your grown-up body. Your trapezius tightens, the levator scapulae stiffens in silent protest, and your breath shortens to keep you small and unseen. This isn’t just social conditioning; it’s muscle memory. The floor holds all the places where your body was told to shrink, hush, and not take up space. When you lower yourself down, you’re disrupting decades of subtle tension patterns—your jaw clenches, your psoas resists lengthening, the skin along your ribs feels unfamiliar. This discomfort is not a failure. It’s your body’s way of saying, *I remember*. The floor is not beneath you; it is a threshold.   

**Q: My knees and hips protest loudly when I try floor work. Isn’t this just too much for my body?**  
A: Your body is talking, yes. But that voice is often muffled by years of ignoring the subtle language of fascia and joint capsules. Sharp pain is a clear stop sign, but the nagging ache or stiffness in your knees or hips—the hip flexors tightening, the medial meniscus whispering complaints—that’s your invitation to listen, not quit. The floor doesn’t have to be your enemy. Try easing into supported postures: a folded blanket under your ischial tuberosities, a bolster beneath your knees, or a rolled towel along your lumbar spine. Herbs like comfrey ointment can also be massaged into the joints before you begin. The goal is not to force your body into submission, but to offer it a supported way to remember the ground.

**Q: How does floor work even connect to anger? I don’t see the link.**  
A: The connection is etched in your fascia and muscle tone, a silent dialogue between your nervous system and the ground beneath you. When anger arises, your diaphragm tightens, your masseter clenches, and your psoas contracts like a coiled spring. Sitting or lying on the floor invites these muscles to release their clenched stories. You’re giving your body permission to move downward without shame, to feel the weight of your own presence fully. Resmaa Menakem teaches that trauma and rage lodge in the body’s edges—your feet, your hands, your hips—and the floor is the first place you can meet those edges without judgment. This is not about forcing anger out or controlling it; it’s about listening to the body’s message before the fury turns to fire.  

**Q: I’m afraid if I do floor work, I’ll get stuck in old pain or memories. How do I protect myself?**  
A: This is where somatic guidance and herbal allies become your trusted companions. You don’t have to dive headfirst into old wounds. Start with just a few minutes, observing the subtle shifts in your diaphragm or the gentle release in your masseter. Pema Chödrön reminds us that sitting with discomfort doesn’t mean drowning in it. Use grounding herbs like valerian or skullcap to calm the nervous system before practice, and keep a journal nearby to anchor your experience with words after. If emotions rise too fiercely, a simple shift—rolling to your side, pressing your feet into the floor, or rising slowly—can bring you back to safety. The floor is a witness, not a trap.  

**Q: I don’t have the flexibility or strength to do “floor work.” Is it really for me?**  
A: Flexibility is a myth sold by Instagram gurus. The floor is for every body, not just the bendy ones. It’s about presence, not performance. Even if your hamstrings feel like taut steel cables or your shoulders rebel with every inch lowered, your body remembers how to soften. Start small: a seated breath practice with your sit bones grounded, or a simple lying twist with knees supported. The body’s fascia remodels slowly, like the patient work of an herbal poultice drawing out infection. Trust the process, not the timeline. Gabor Maté reminds us that the body knows what it needs; your job is to listen, no matter how loud the resistance.  

In every creak and ache, in every moment you resist, the floor waits—steady, unyielding, and ready to hold your fury with the quiet patience of earth itself.
